events.content:
[{"role": "question", "text": "Andrew Strzelczyk [2570]: Yeah, sure. Yeah, absolutely. So where are you really seeing the growth? Is it in peak weekend? Is it during the week? Is it earlier? Yeah. Sorry. Yep.", "speaker": "Andrew Strzelczyk", "title": "Analyst, Bank of Montreal"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Balin [2581]: Right. Appreciate that. It's Michael. You know, as I look at it, we're seeing growth, you know, seven days a week. We're seeing it, you know, early in the day. We're seeing it late at night. We're seeing it during our power hours. So really strong growth throughout, you know, all days of the week. Maybe it's a little bit stronger on weekends on a percentage basis, but very pleased to see that it's coming, you know, across the board. which, again, I think that's guest learning where there's opportunity to get into a Texas Roadhouse and managing their schedules there. As far as the decline in to-go, yeah, it's down a little bit on a percentage basis, but that is largely due to the increase in our dining room visits and our dining room sales. We still did over $19,000 per store week in to-go sales in Q1, which was only down a few hundred dollars versus Q1 of last year. And yeah, probably some of those guests are now coming into the dining room and dining with us.", "speaker": "Michael Balin", "title": "Head of Investor Relations, Texas Roadhouse"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Andy Barish [2667]: Hey, guys. Just a couple of housekeeping and maybe an operational question. Michael, on the G&A guide, are you including the 2.6 one-timer? I mean, obviously, on an annualized basis, that would be an additional $10 million or so for the G&A.", "speaker": "Andy Barish", "title": "Analyst, Jefferies"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Balin [2692]: I think you could probably take that out and then make it \u2013", "speaker": "Michael Balin", "title": "Head of Investor Relations, Texas Roadhouse"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Andy Barish [2696]: more in line again we'll see what where the actual you know gna comes in but typically i think it's fair to assume if you strip that out then you know that would be the more comparable number to use as you move through the year yeah just wanted to make sure and then um did did april you know have some of the volatility around calendar spring breaks and some of your markets that we've heard from others and how does How does average weekly sales seasonality usually progress from, you know, the 145 in April to May and June?", "speaker": "Andy Barish", "title": "Analyst, Jefferies"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Balin [2734]: Yeah, I mean, you know, there may have been some, you know, seasonal movements, you know, from week to week in those April numbers, but we still saw strength, overall strength throughout the, you know, throughout the month, and so it's not um that we were strong early on and then things fell off or vice versa so uh there were there were mismatches but uh still felt like we saw strength throughout if you you know typically now moving you know you know obviously april tends to be a very you know strong month for us and then you know may can be a strong month as well with uh mother's day you know june july august Even into September, those summer months, we see the seasonality come in and maybe our volumes are a little bit lower than what you would see early in the year. And then they begin to climb back up into the fall and winter months.", "speaker": "Michael Balin", "title": "Head of Investor Relations, Texas Roadhouse"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Andy Barish [2800]: Very helpful. And then, Jerry, on the labor hour increases, I'm assuming that's both you know, back of house and front, are there some things, you know, you can look at in terms of, I know the kind of the KDS that you're looking at, anything equipment-wise or changes in the back that, you know, may help as well?", "speaker": "Andy Barish", "title": "Analyst, Jefferies"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Jerry Morgan [2827]: Andy, how you doing? Yeah, I think we're looking at every component as to how to become more efficient. The number one thing is being fully staffed. and really holding on to our people and keeping that tenure so their productivity really increases. And maybe you can use a less component on a prep or that because of their efficiency. So we'll continue to look at that as we've really ramped back up our staffing. You know, our component is a heavy staffing. We have made from scratch food, homemade, obviously, from that side of it. We have a lot of commitment to legendary food and legendary service. And as the more experience that they get, the more efficient and productive they can be. And we should see that continue to pay dividends for us as we've invested heavily over the last couple of years to get to that level that we really want to be executing at a high level always. And that is obviously driving our top line mentality and the way we've been able to conduct business for a large portion of our time in the business.", "speaker": "Jerry Morgan", "title": "Chief Executive Officer, Texas Roadhouse"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Jim Sanderson [2907]: Hey, thanks for the question. I wanted to go back to labor just to make sure I understand the incremental or the growth in labor hours reported in the first quarter. Is that a rate that will continue? it's going to be a little bit higher than you would typically see as you get to a recovery phase relative to, you know, last year in COVID disruptions, the 5%.", "speaker": "Jim Sanderson", "title": "Analyst, North Coast Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Balin [2928]: Hey, Jeff, this is Michael. I would say Q1 is the anomaly with, again, one, the lapping of Omicron and, you know, our staffing levels in 2022 were at their lowest in Q1 of last year, specifically early in the pandemic. quarter. So I think your biggest guest hour, I'm sorry, labor hours growth, you know, was in that Q1. But I'd also point out, you know, that 4.8% labor hour growth goes along with 7.6% traffic growth. So the ratio there is not that far off of what we, you know, would try to get to in the past. But as we move through the year, again, a piece of it will have to do with what happens on the top line. But if traffic remains healthy, we continue to grow, I would expect you will continue to see some labor hour growth, not necessarily at that rate. If you think about how we talked about the business over the course of 2022, on the calls, we would say, hey, our staffing has gotten better, but there are still some pockets of opportunity. And a quarter later, we'd say, Those pockets have become smaller, so we still have to lap that this year with what we think are getting to be some very strong staffing levels, but the year-over-year comparisons will result in some labor hour growth. Again, assuming the business continues to move in the right direction, but Q1 should absolutely be the highest point for the year.", "speaker": "Michael Balin", "title": "Head of Investor Relations, Texas Roadhouse"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Jim Sanderson [3027]: right thank you for that and just a quick follow-up question on pricing i think you've outlined how it looks to me as if mid pricing will be pretty much mid single digits until fourth quarter how does that compare or stack up against your competitors in the steak segment or casual dining is the gap with competitors similar is it narrowing just how you look at that yeah and and first just on that fourth quarter you know we will look at pricing again", "speaker": "Jim Sanderson", "title": "Analyst, North Coast Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Michael Balin [3055]: I would expect we'd do something in that October period which would make that 2.9 maybe potentially be more in line with those other quarters, but we'll see what we do. I would say that we think our pricing, if you look at NAP information, I think our pricing is below what others have done. We've done what we felt is right for our business, focusing on taking pricing to offset some of the structural inflation that we're feeling, but keeping the guests at top of mind and making sure they still feel like they're getting a value when they're coming into Texas Roadhouse. So I would say that our pricing actions have been below our competitors, and I think some of the NAP data would confirm that.", "speaker": "Michael Balin", "title": "Head of Investor Relations, Texas Roadhouse"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Jerry Morgan [3116]: Thank you very much. I appreciate you all being with us. And I just want to close in thanking all of Rhody Nation out there as we celebrated our 30 year anniversary and the tremendous success that we've had. It's because of each and every one of you committed to legendary food and legendary service. Everyone have a great evening and thank you for your time.", "speaker": "Jerry Morgan", "title": "Chief Executive Officer, Texas Roadhouse"}]
